narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, like your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and plans to attend full-time. For you specifically, it estimated a 45% probability that you would fail the bar exam—high enough that the university decided to reject your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of success. The decision hinged on just a few standout factors from your profile, with your Year 3 decile ranking (a 2, well below the typical 5.5 average) being the most influential, as it strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar failure compared to most applicants. Pushing in the opposite direction, your solid Year 1 decile of 9 (above the average 5.7) worked in your favor, suggesting early potential that tempered the prediction somewhat. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 (slightly under the 3.2 average) also helped by nudging the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass, though not enough to overcome the top concern. While your LSAT score of 27 (below the usual 37) and full-time enrollment plans aligned with the majority of students, they didn't sway the outcome much here. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having a strong start but a notable dip later on, which the model weighed heavily as a red flag for bar performance. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a lawyer—it's just the model's data-driven read on patterns from similar backgrounds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that address these gaps. Many students in your position have succeeded by targeting these areas, and universities often value persistence and improvement. You're clearly capable, given your early grades, so focusing here could flip the script for your next application. Keep pushing—you've got this.
